parts.content:
**The human face of student counselling**

Many Diploma Programme (DP) coordinators are finding they do not have enough time to interview every student before they choose their DP subjects. They are considering purchasing the Student Counselling System, a proprietary management information system (MIS).

The developers of the Student Counselling System claim that, if students complete online questionnaires to determine their interests, abilities and future goals, the system can give them very accurate results, resulting in less than 5% of students changing their diploma subjects. They also claim the Student Counselling System will allow DP coordinators to be able to interview every student, something they cannot do now.

Some DP coordinators have concerns about the automation of this process and believe that advising students is something that is best done by relying on their own professional judgement and intuition.

Discuss whether DP coordinators should rely on the recommendations of the Student Counselling System rather than their own professional judgement and intuition when advising students about what DP subjects to study.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Reasons why DP coordinators should rely on the recommendations of the Student Counselling System (claim):**

*   If a large number of decisions based on a wide range of scenarios are programmed into the Student Counselling System, it should give accurate and reliable results (systems, feasibility).
*   Students may be able to carry out initial investigations themselves to determine whether their proposed choices are realistic (judgement).
*   If many of the scenarios are similar, the DP coordinator may be able to see a number of students together to give general advice, making the process more efficient.
*   Students may appreciate the consistency / transparency of the information provided by the Student Counselling System (equity).
*   In many cases, the scenario may be relatively straightforward, and the Student Counselling System may be able to provide consistent information that the DP coordinators would have in a fraction of the time, allowing them to focus on the students who do have atypical requirements (feasibility, costs).

**Reasons why DP coordinators should rely on their judgement (counter-claim):**

*   DP coordinators know that special circumstances / family issues / personal information can have an impact on the decision and this will require their professional judgement, intuition, valuesandethics.
*   They may encounter scenarios that are not part of the system, which limit its usability / feasibility.
*   DP coordinators might be aware of new subjects/majors that have not been updated into the system (change).
*   Students may have multiple interests, and the Student Counselling System cannot give accurate recommendations.
*   Giving career advice is too important to be left to the algorithms that exist within the Student Counselling System (values, ethics).

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:**automation, change, power, systems, values, ethics, judgement, intuition, consistency, transparency, efficiency, algorithm, accuracy, reliability, equity, costs, feasibility

parts.content: Identify two components of the World Wide Web and describe their functions.
parts.markscheme:
### Components of the World Wide Web

- <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a valid component, such as:
  - Web browser (e.g. Chrome, Firefox, Safari) - used to access and display web pages
  - Web server - stores and serves web pages/content to clients
  - URL (Uniform Resource Locator) - address used to locate web pages
  - HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) - protocol for transmitting data over the web
  - HTML (Hypertext Markup Language) - language used to create web pages

- <marks>1 mark</marks> for a valid description of the function of the identified component

<note>Award marks for any other valid and relevant components and their functions</note>

parts.content: Outline the role of URLs in accessing web resources.
parts.markscheme:
URLs (Uniform Resource Locators) play a crucial role in accessing web resources. Here is an outline of their role:

- URLs provide a standardized way to identify and locate resources on the internet <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They specify the protocol (e.g., http, https) to be used to access the resource <marks>1 mark</marks>
- URLs contain the domain name or IP address of the web server hosting the resource <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They include the path to the specific resource (e.g., file, webpage) on the server  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how search engines use web protocols to help users find information.
parts.markscheme:
To access websites, search engines use the HTTP/HTTPS protocols:
- HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is used to request and transmit data over the internet <marks>1 mark</marks>
- HTTPS (HTTP Secure) is the secure version, encrypting the data for privacy <marks>1 mark</marks>

Search engines also use other protocols:
- DNS (Domain Name System) to translate domain names to IP addresses <marks>1 mark</marks>
$$
\text{e.g. www.example.com} \rightarrow \text{192.168.0.1}
$$

- Robots.txt file specifies which parts of a website should not be accessed by web crawlers <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the impact of the web on information accessibility, considering both the benefits of easy access and the risks of misinformation.
parts.markscheme:
### Possible Benefits of Easy Access

- Vast amount of information available online from various sources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ability to access information from anywhere with an internet connection <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Democratization of knowledge, making information accessible to a wider audience <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Risks of Misinformation

- Lack of quality control or fact-checking for online information <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spread of false or misleading information (e.g., fake news, conspiracy theories) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Difficulty in verifying the credibility and accuracy of online sources <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Balanced Evaluation

- Recognize the advantages of easy access to information for education, research, and personal growth <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acknowledge the potential dangers of misinformation and the need for critical thinking and fact-checking <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Candidates should provide a well-rounded analysis considering both the benefits and risks, and demonstrate an understanding of the impact of the web on information accessibility.</note>

parts.content:
_CBR Bank_ holds a large amount of information on its customers. Some customers are concerned about the security, privacy and anonymity of their data.

For **each** of the concerns above, explain**one** policy that _CBR Bank_ could use to address the concerns of its customers.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Security:**

*   User access to data is limited to authorized personnel – to ensure data is secure during storage.
*   Username and password access is implemented – to ensure data is secure during storage.
*   A password policy is implemented (e.g., minimum length, mix of characters, changed after a given number of days etc_._).
*   Two-factor / two-step authentication is implemented (e.g., one-time password/PIN to a mobile phone, code-generating device supplied by the bank, confirmation email).
*   Data is encrypted – to ensure data is secure during transmission.
*   A firewall is used to protect the bank’s server.
*   Bank servers are kept updated with the latest software / security patches.
*   Bank employees are prohibited from accessing customer data on personal devices / devices outside the company network – to ensure that all devices are protected by the bank’s security measures.

**Privacy:**

*   Customers are informed to specify how the data may be used – if/how it may be shared with third parties.
*   Only authorized personnel will have access to the bank database – not all employees will be able to view data.

**Anonymity:**

*   Ensure the customer’s anonymity is maintained – when data is shared with third parties, the data that could give the identity of a customer must be detached.
*   Reports are kept anonymous – reports cannot allow individuals to be identified.

**_N.B.:_** _T__he response requires an explanation of a policy and not a discussion of the problems themselves. There must be a policy for **each** kind of concern: security, privacy and anonymity and reason(s)._

_Award **\[1\]** for identifying a policy that CBR Bank could use to address the security, privacy and anonymity concerns of its customers and **\[1\]** for a development of the policy identified up to a maximum of **\[2\]**._

_Mark as **\[2\] + \[2\] + \[2\]**._

parts.content:
The chief executive officer (CEO) of _CBR Bank_, Alice McEwan, said in a recent interview, “_CBR Bank_ will be replacing all passwords, PINs and personal verification questions for our online banking and mobile banking with voice biometrics recognition.”

To what extent are the changes proposed by Alice beneficial for both _CBR Bank_’s customers **and** _CBR Bank_’s IT support?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**For customers:**

**Advantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   Customers don’t have to remember a password or PIN code / may not need additional verification such as one-time codes (automation).
*   It is more secure, as voice characteristics are unique.
*   It is harder for others to hack online banking with voice biometrics recognition (security).
*   Some customers may have physical conditions that make entering PINs/passwords difficult – voice recognition will avoid having to type (access/inclusion).

**Disadvantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   The voice recognition system may not accept foreign accents or a range of voices (systems).
*   Illness (such as a cold) can change a person’s voice, making identification difficult (systems).
*   A person’s voice can be easily recorded and used for unauthorized access (security).
*   Someone with very similar voices (e.g., a member of the same family) may be able to gain access to the bank account (security).

**For IT support:**

**Advantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   It is more secure, less likely to be hacked – fewer problems for IT staff to deal with (automation, systems).
*   It is easy for customers to record by themselves – no IT staff required to set up (automation, systems).
*   IT staff do not have to deal with lost password/PIN (automation, systems).

**Disadvantages of replacing passwords with biometric voice recognition:**

*   The voice recognition system may not accept particular accents – customers cannot access their online banking and will need support (systems).
*   A person’s voice can be easily recorded and used for unauthorized access – customers may complain about unauthorized access, IT staff will have to investigate hacked accounts (security, values).
*   Illness (such as a cold) can change a person’s voice, making identification difficult – a greater number of customers might need to call support to access their own account (systems).
*   When the new system is implemented, _CBR_ _Bank_’s IT support could be overwhelmed with overlooked bugs (reliability).
*   Audio files / biometric templates will require more storage space than passwords / PINs – this may make backing up data more time consuming / require IT support to increase available storage space, etc (data).
*   Initial implementation of the new system may require additional IT support staff.
*   IT support staff may face an increased workload (e.g., if the old system initially has to run parallel to the new system).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** security, authentication, stakeholder, reliability, data, security, change, identity, power, systems, values, ethics, accountability, transparency, access, inclusion

parts.content:
**A cashless society**

In the near future, it is possible that cash will not be accepted as a means of payment in Sweden. People are already using alternative ways of paying, such as mobile payment, card payment and internet payment. Currently, over 95% of citizens in Sweden have internet access.

Many people in Sweden claim there are advantages of using an app developed by Swish. The Swish app allows friends to share a restaurant bill, pay where credit or debit cards are not accepted, for babysitting or parking tickets, or make a donation at church.

However, other people in Sweden claim that making the Swish app the only means of payment may increase inequalities within the country.

Discuss whether countries should pass legislation making apps such as Swish the only means of payment.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Benefits of apps such as Swish _being_ the only form of payment (claim)**

*   No need to carry cash (digitalization) / credit cards – no risk of being stolen/no risk of not having enough cash.
*   No risk of credit card being used fraudulently (values).
*   Transactions are recorded – there is proof of payment, and the system should increase transparency / acceptability.
*   Payments are made immediately – no need to wait until person has time to go to bank to get cash (feasibility).
*   Can solve other problems regarding money – bills can be shared, one person pays, and money is transferred (feasibility).
*   In an emergency, money can be transferred to dependents without them being close (e.g., children at university) (feasibility).
*   Allows money transactions between individuals.
*   Can limit the amount of money to be transferred, thus preventing individuals spending more than they have available (acceptability / feasibility).
*   Many people are already used to the app, so it would be a good choice if the country were going cashless (acceptability).
*   Easier for users to track budgets / spending, as all transactions are digital (transparency).

**Disadvantages of apps such as Swish _being_ the only form of payment (counter-claim)**

*   Swedish banks will be able to obtain more data on their users’ transaction habits (privacy concern, ethics, values).
*   Is not available to people who do not have a bank account, so a potential digital divide concern (equity).
*   May be problematic for tourists who may not have the app or who cannot link the purchase to their bank account (systems, equity).
*   Removes the anonymity of the payee in transactions – the app may store a user’s transaction history. This would include date, item, recipient of the money and cost of the item (values, ethics).
*   The bank controls (power) the maximum amount of money that can be transferred, which may limit a person’s spending and may not be appropriate in certain situations (acceptability).
*   It may not be technically possible to make the transition from a society that uses cash for transactions to one that does not (change, systems).
*   If a single app is used, it would give Swish an unfair monopoly over the technology (power, values, ethics).
*   Digital divide – smartphone ownership and use by mature adults.
*   If a person loses their phone, breaks it, or its battery runs out, they have no way to pay for anything (systems, equity).
*   Failure in / lack of phone network coverage could affect when and where people could use the app (systems, equity).
*   Failure of the system / technical issues / down time would prevent people from making transactions (systems, feasibility).

_Accept implicit and explicit references to apps such as the Swish app._

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** business, families, digital divide, access, inclusion, acceptability, feasibility, equity, digitalization, anonymity, privacy, change, power, systems, values, ethics

parts.content: Identify two ethical dilemmas associated with AI and describe their implications.
parts.markscheme:
### Ethical Dilemma 1: Bias and Discrimination

AI systems can perpetuate and amplify societal biases present in the training data, leading to unfair and discriminatory outcomes. This can result in:
- Biased decision-making in areas like hiring, lending, and criminal justice
- Reinforcing existing prejudices and stereotypes
- Disproportionately disadvantaging certain groups

### Ethical Dilemma 2: Privacy and Surveillance

The massive data collection and processing capabilities of AI raise privacy concerns:
- Potential for unauthorized access, misuse, or exploitation of personal data
- Erosion of individual privacy through pervasive surveillance and monitoring
- Lack of transparency and control over how personal data is used

### Ethical Dilemma 3: Accountability and Transparency

AI systems can be opaque "black boxes," making it difficult to understand their decision-making processes:
- Lack of accountability for errors or harmful outcomes
- Difficulty in assigning responsibility and liability
- Challenges in ensuring fairness, explainability, and due process

### Ethical Dilemma 4: Automation and Job Displacement

The increasing automation enabled by AI could lead to significant job losses and economic disruption:
- Potential for mass unemployment and income inequality
- Challenges in retraining and reskilling the workforce
- Societal and economic impacts on individuals and communities

<marks>2 marks for identifying and describing any two ethical dilemmas associated with AI</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the ethical challenges of automation in AI, particularly regarding job displacement and economic impacts.

### Key Points
- Automation and AI can lead to job displacement and economic disruption <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Potential negative economic impacts <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Wealth inequality may increase as profits concentrate with companies deploying AI
  - Workers may struggle to find new employment or retrain for different roles
  - Overall economic productivity could decline during the transition period

### Ethical Considerations
- Balancing technological progress with protecting workers' livelihoods <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - AI automation offers potential efficiency gains but at what human cost?
  - Ethical obligation to mitigate negative impacts and support displaced workers

- Ensuring a fair transition and equitable distribution of benefits <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Policies and support systems needed for retraining and job placement
  - Mechanisms to share economic gains from AI across society
  - Potential for universal basic income or other social safety nets

<note>Responses should address both the economic impacts of automation as well as the key ethical tradeoffs and considerations involved. Well-rounded answers weighing different perspectives are expected.</note>

Explain how routers and switches work together to support connectivity within a network.
parts.markscheme:
- Routers connect multiple networks together and route data packets between them <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Switches connect devices within the same network and forward data packets to the intended destination <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Routers:} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Operate at the network layer (layer 3) of the OSI model} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Use IP addresses to determine where to forward packets} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Connect different networks (e.g., LAN to WAN)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Switches:} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Operate at the data link layer (layer 2) of the OSI model} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Use MAC addresses to forward packets within the same network} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Connect devices in the same LAN}
\end{aligned}
$$

- Routers and switches work together to enable communication across networks <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Switches connect devices within a LAN
  - Routers connect LANs to other networks (e.g., the internet)

parts.content: Discuss the implications of AI-driven automation on employment, focusing on the balance between economic efficiency and potential job displacement.
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of AI-driven automation and its general effect on employment.
- Limited discussion on economic efficiency or job displacement, with little depth or balance between the two.
- Few examples or specifics to illustrate key points.


"AI-driven automation can lead to job loss in some industries."
"Automation can make work faster and more efficient."
</commonMistake>

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear explanation of AI-driven automation's dual impact on employment, including both potential economic efficiency and job displacement.
- Some discussion of benefits (e.g., improved productivity and cost reduction) alongside basic mention of risks (e.g., job loss in specific sectors).
- Examples provided to support points, though may lack depth or variety.


"AI-driven automation increases economic efficiency by speeding up processes and reducing costs, which benefits businesses."
"However, certain jobs, especially in manufacturing and repetitive tasks, may be at risk of displacement."
Examples might include references to automation in retail (self-checkout systems) or manufacturing (robotic assembly lines).
</commonMistake>

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- In-depth evaluation of the implications of AI-driven automation on employment, considering both economic efficiency and job displacement.
- Balanced analysis of benefits and risks, showing nuanced understanding of how automation can drive economic growth but also result in challenges for workforce adaptation.
- Detailed examination of sectors where automation is most impactful (e.g., manufacturing, retail, transportation) and where new opportunities may emerge (e.g., tech support, AI maintenance).
- Examples used effectively to illustrate points, with insights into both the positive impacts (e.g., efficiency, growth in new sectors) and negative impacts (e.g., job displacement, need for reskilling).

$$
\text{Economic Efficiency} \leftrightarrow \text{Job Displacement}
$$


"AI-driven automation enhances economic efficiency by reducing labor costs and increasing productivity, allowing companies to produce goods and services at lower prices, potentially increasing consumer access."
"However, automation can displace jobs in sectors where repetitive tasks are common. For instance, autonomous vehicles could reduce the demand for truck drivers, and robotic process automation could decrease the need for administrative roles."
"There is also a shift in job creation, with new roles emerging in AI development, maintenance, and oversight, but this requires significant retraining and may not offset losses in low-skill jobs."
</commonMistake>

<examTip>Balancing these effects is difficult, as job displacement impacts socio-economic stability. Policymakers might need to consider interventions such as retraining programs, universal basic income, or support for sectors heavily affected by automation.</examTip>

parts.content: To what extent does bandwidth availability impact the quality of online activities like streaming and gaming, considering both user experience and network limitations?
parts.markscheme:
- Bandwidth is the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection in a given time period <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher bandwidth allows for faster data transfer rates and better quality for online activities like:
    - Video streaming (higher resolution, less buffering) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Online gaming (lower latency, smoother gameplay) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Video conferencing (higher quality audio/video) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- User experience is impacted by bandwidth limitations:
    - Low bandwidth leads to poor quality video/audio, lag, buffering <marks>1 mark</marks>
    <commonMistake>Confusing bandwidth with internet speed</commonMistake>

- Network limitations that impact bandwidth availability:
    - Type of internet connection (e.g. fiber optic vs DSL) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Distance from internet service provider <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Network congestion during peak usage times <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Shared bandwidth among multiple users/devices <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Solutions to increase bandwidth:
    - Upgrade to a higher-tier internet plan <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Use wired Ethernet instead of WiFi <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Optimize home network (e.g. better router placement) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**App or intuition?**

Apps have been developed that allow a customer to submit a trip request to a taxi company on their smartphone. These apps allow the customer to select the starting point and final destination of the journey as well as add other information, such as the time of journey and number of passengers.

The developers of the taxi journey app claim it can capture the real-time details of a journey, including the starting point, route, distance travelled, the speed of the car and the final destination. The app integrates this information with real-time traffic information, such as closed roads or accidents. Fares can be estimated before the journey, and the customer can pay digitally with their credit card or by PayPal.

Some traditional taxi drivers in cities such as Paris and Rio de Janeiro have campaigned against the introduction of these apps in taxi companies. Based on previous journeys recorded by the app, the taxi company recommends that their taxi drivers rely on the taxi journey app rather than their own knowledge of the city when determining the best route.

Discuss whether the taxi driver should rely on the taxi journey app, rather than their own knowledge of the city, when determining the best route.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Rely on the taxi journey app (claim)**

*   It will speed up the process of drivers being able to learn the routes (automation), meaning they are able to provide a better service to customers more quickly.
*   The app will give consistency in the routes used, so the passengers should be confident that the route being selected is the most appropriate (trust, transparency).
*   If the decisions of the route in the app are collected, the results may be analysed and the reliability evaluated/increased. It may make it possible to monitor the drivers more closely.
*   The app may be updated in real time, so the driver may not need to check on changes such as roads being closed or reopening (change).
*   The app may result in shorter distances being travelled and cost savings for the taxi company (cost).
*   The driver may not have sufficient knowledge of these areas to be able to determine an effective route.

**Rely on driver’s knowledge and intuition (counter-claim)**

*   They may use their professional judgement / intuition that has been gained by doing the job for a number of years.
*   The system may not be able to determine certain routes, such as those that go outside of the central part of the city. This may mean the usability / effectiveness / reliability of the app is reduced.
*   There may be new requirements that have not been incorporated into the system (change) that may mean it is not providing the most up-to-date information (cost).
*   The route-finding algorithms that exist within the digital system may have inherent/subconscious biases (power, values, ethics). For example, they may route the driver past certain locations, or avoid others.
*   There may be parts of the city where the connection cannot be maintained, such as where there are very tall buildings surrounding the road. This may lead to the app not performing as expected (reliability, feasibility).
*   The taxi driver may feel that they are being surveilled (power, values, ethics).
*   Would the installation of the app be a requirement for the job (systems, ethics, values)?

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

### AI and Transparency in Decision-Making
AI is used in critical areas such as finance, healthcare, and criminal justice, where decision-making transparency is crucial. ""Black box"" algorithms, however, lack transparency, making it difficult for users to understand or challenge AI-driven decisions, which can affect trust and fairness.

parts.content: To what extent should transparency be a priority in AI-driven decision-making, particularly in high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice?
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of transparency in AI.
- Limited discussion on why transparency might be important, with minimal reference to high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice.
- Few or no examples to support points.

Example Points:

- "Transparency is needed to see how AI decisions are made."
- "It is important in high-stakes areas like finance."

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear explanation of the concept of transparency and its importance in AI decision-making.
- Some discussion of high-stakes areas (finance, criminal justice), mentioning the potential consequences of opaque AI systems.
- Basic mention of risks in not prioritizing transparency, such as accountability and fairness issues.
- Some examples to illustrate key points, though may lack depth.

Example Points:

- "Transparency in AI is crucial for accountability, especially in areas like criminal justice where lives can be directly affected."
- "In finance, a lack of transparency could lead to unfair credit decisions, impacting people's financial stability."
- Examples may include basic references to credit scoring or criminal sentencing decisions influenced by AI without deep analysis.

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- In-depth evaluation of the necessity of transparency in AI, particularly in high-stakes fields.
- Comprehensive discussion of ethical and practical implications, including how transparency supports accountability, trust, and fairness.
- Detailed analysis of risks associated with opaque AI systems, such as potential bias, wrongful decisions, and the challenges to correcting errors without transparency.
- Balanced argument with consideration of challenges to implementing transparency, like trade-offs with proprietary information or the complexity of AI models.
- Use of specific examples to illustrate implications in both finance (e.g., credit scoring or loan approvals) and criminal justice (e.g., sentencing algorithms or predictive policing).

Example Points:

- "Transparency in AI decision-making is essential in high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice, where decisions have far-reaching impacts on individuals' lives. It ensures that affected parties can understand, contest, and seek recourse for decisions that impact them."
- "In criminal justice, lack of transparency in AI-driven sentencing algorithms can lead to biased outcomes without accountability, eroding public trust. Transparency is needed to scrutinize whether the system perpetuates existing biases, particularly against marginalized groups."
- "In finance, opaque algorithms used in credit scoring can disadvantage certain applicants without providing a clear reason, limiting access to financial opportunities."
- <note>Challenges: Achieving transparency can be difficult when models are complex or proprietary, but efforts like explainable AI and regulatory oversight could help balance transparency with innovation.</note>

questions.specification:
**Technology disruption in Orams Academy**

Many schools use virtual learning environments (VLEs), such as Firefly, PowerSchool Learning, Schoology, Final Site LMS, and Google Classroom. The VLE is used for document storage, lesson content, and homework management as well as for communication between the school, students and parents.

In addition to the VLE, collaborative cloud-based online tools, such as Google G Suite and Microsoft Office 365, are used by students and teachers.

Recently, Daniela Stark, the principal at Orams Academy, has become concerned that using only one VLE may not prove to be as beneficial as originally thought. She has heard that some teachers and students are also successfully using social media tools, such as Facebook, Twitter and Instagram, to share their learning. She has also been told that the lack of bandwidth that the school experiences at times can cause problems.

Daniela is considering an alternative approach, allowing teachers and students to choose the most appropriate tools to manage their learning.

parts.content:
The increasing use of cloud-based applications and online collaborative tools has led to Orams Academy introducing an acceptableuse policy for students and teachers.

Explain**three** reasons why Orams Academy decided to introduce an acceptable use policy.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

*   To establish a philosophy of Orams Academy relating to the use of online environments…
*   which acts as a guiding statement and provides the overarching principles for online behaviour (values).  
    

*   To establish a code of conduct (values)…
*   that provides clear and unambiguous directions about how staff and students should act while online / using appropriate language in text chats / avoiding bullying behaviour / avoiding copying or plagiarism.  
      
    

*   To outline sanctions…
*   that would be imposed if staff or students did not adhere to the code of conduct (values).  
      
    

*   To establish a disclaimer…
*   so that Orams Academy is not responsible for the actions of the staff or students if an illegal activity is carried out / managers at Orams Academy are able to supervise everything that is posted onto the VLE / managers at Orams Academy would be able to read students’ messages (spaces).  
      
    

*   To establish data privacy and protection guidelines (security)…
*   so it will be transparent who owns the data created in the VLE, for how long, and what type of data is collected by the VLE servers (systems).

_Award **\[1\]** for each reason given and an additional **\[1\]** for a development of this reason up to a maximum of **\[2\]** marks._

_Mark as **\[2\]** \+ **\[2\]** \+ **\[2\]**._

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** cloud, policy, security, ubiquity, digital media, remote learning, change, spaces, systems, values

parts.content: Discuss whether Daniela should make every teacher at Orams Academy use the same learning platform **or** allow each teacher to choose their own preferred learning management approach.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**A single learning platform:**

*   This means that all of the information is centralized and can be easily accessed by staff, students and parents (systems).
*   The training requirements for staff are reduced, as they only have to use one system (systems).
*   It prevents the use of a range of formats that may be incompatible, meaning that resources cannot be shared, or may need adapting to work on a different platform (systems).
*   Having a common learning platform would make it convenient for the IT department to take backup/restore and roll over data to new academic year (systems).
*   It makes it easier for senior managers to monitor the actions of staff, view lessons, etc., for consistency (values, power).
*   Can be integrated into any acceptable use policy or training requirements (values).
*   May be costly and based on proprietary software, meaning that fixes could be expensive and take a long time to happen (change).
*   If the single platform is inaccessible for any reason, all learning content would be inaccessible (systems).

**Teachers choosing their own preferred learning management approach:**

*   allows teachers to use their expertise of particular tools
*   allows teachers to feel they are being allowed to use their creativity and innovative approach to the teaching and learning (power)
*   may integrate with social media tools that may be blocked by the school and provide a better user experience
*   may provide better functionality than a “one size fits all” approach
*   may lead to staff or students working outside an acceptable use policy / using the sites inappropriately
*   the training requirements for staff will be increased if the staff are unfamiliar with the platforms / training will only occur on an _ad hoc_ basis / training might be decreased since staff are already familiar with the tools they choose to use
*   expertise of a platform may depend on particular teachers – if they leave, then no one may be able to support students with this tool (systems).

**Keywords:** business, learning, education, training, judgement, platform, monitoring, acceptable use policy, interoperability, policy, change, systems, values

questions.specification:
### Source A: The Evolution and Impact of Different Types of Computers
Computers have diversified significantly, ranging from large-scale mainframes that handle intensive processing tasks to wearable devices like fitness trackers that support everyday activities. Each type serves unique functions across industries.

For example, mainframe computers power large databases and transactions in banking, processing millions of transactions in seconds. Smart devices, such as mobile phones, have also transformed digital communication, offering constant connectivity to the internet and applications.

### Source B: Components of a Computer: Hardware, Interfaces, and Software
A computer’s functionality relies on the interplay of hardware, interfaces, and software. The central processing unit (CPU) acts as the “brain” of the system, while memory stores data temporarily and storage components hold data permanently. User interfaces enable interaction, and software executes tasks and provides structure.

For instance, a smartphone’s CPU works in tandem with its operating system and touchscreen to enable fast user responses and efficient multitasking, providing a seamless experience in everyday tasks.


### Source C: Computer Coding and Programming Languages
Programming languages allow humans to communicate instructions to computers. From foundational languages like Fortran and C to modern languages like Python and JavaScript, each has specific rules and structures. Coding’s flexibility allows it to be applied in diverse fields, from software development to artificial intelligence.

For instance, Python’s readability has made it a popular choice in data science, allowing researchers to analyze data and create machine learning algorithms effectively.


### Source D: The Evolution of Computing: From First Generation to Quantum Computing

parts.content: With reference to Sources A–D and your own knowledge, evaluate how the evolution of computers has impacted modern industries. Include examples from at least two industries.
parts.markscheme:
Sample:
### Introduction

The evolution of computers—from mainframes to quantum technology—has drastically impacted industries, enhancing productivity, accuracy, and innovation across various sectors.

### Industry Examples

#### Finance

Mainframe computers support extensive data processing, allowing banks to conduct high-frequency transactions and manage customer databases efficiently. Advanced algorithms further enable real-time trading, risk assessment, and fraud detection.

#### Healthcare

The rise of wearable computers has enabled continuous patient monitoring, allowing healthcare providers to gather data on vital signs and activity levels for better patient care. In addition, advancements in machine learning and Python applications support diagnostic tools that analyze patient data, aiding in early detection and treatment planning.

### Technological Evolution and Impact

#### Generational Improvements

The shift from transistors to microprocessors and, potentially, quantum computing has led to faster, more efficient devices, opening up new possibilities for automation, data analysis, and machine learning.

#### Programming and Software

The evolution of programming languages (e.g., from Fortran to Python) has expanded accessibility, enabling industries to develop more complex applications tailored to their needs, such as AI-driven diagnostics in healthcare and financial forecasting tools.

### Conclusion

The continuous evolution of computing technology has enabled industries to streamline operations, achieve higher accuracy, and unlock new innovation areas, demonstrating the transformative power of computer advancement on modern business and society.

parts.content:
**Smartwarehouse**

_Greenwood Industries_’ warehouse uses a smart storage solution for the picking of products from the shelves of the warehouse. Robots are used in this automated picking process.

Currently, these robots are programmed to access a knowledge base\* that enables these robots to use the most efficient route for the picking of products.

_Greenwood Industries_ claims the picking process would be improved if the expert system was replaced by an artificial neural network (ANN).

Discuss whether _Greenwood Industries_ should replace the existing system with one that uses an artificial neural network (ANN).  
  

* * *

\* knowledge base: a set of rules that are manually input into an expert system.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Reasons why _Greenwood Industries_ should replace the expert system with an artificial neural network (ANN) (claim):**

*   May provide greater flexibility in the decision-making process, as knowledge bases only use binary logic (0 or 1, true or false) (algorithms).
*   The ANN may be able to learn and be more flexible than the expert system (systems, innovation).
*   The ANN may be more flexible than using an expert system, as the changes to the knowledge base (or equivalent) will be easier to effect (systems, acceptability).
*   It further automates decision-making – will make the warehouse more efficient (systems, costs).
*   It learns to recognize patterns – adapts to new situations – increase efficiency (systems).
*   Increased accuracy in decision-making – takes into account external factors (systems).
*   The ANN can function if there is some data missing (systems, acceptability).

**Reasons why _Greenwood Industries_ should not replace the expert system with an artificial neural network (ANN) (counter-claim):**

*   It will be harder to implement a new system – so the switch to ANNs will require support (systems, values, feasibility).
*   It will require development costs to introduce a new system that _Greenwood Industries_ may not be able to recoup (systems, costs).
*   The problem may not require an ANN. It may be an example of technological determinism (values).
*   There may not be the hardware / processing capabilities for the ANN (systems, feasibility).
*   Would this lead to further redundancies / increasing automation of employment / loss of human decision-making (ethics, values)?

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** automation, training, costs, algorithms, artificial neural networks, machine learning, automation, change, systems, values, ethics, cost, feasibility

### Source A: Mainframe Computers in Large-Scale Data Processing
Mainframe computers are built to handle high volumes of data and support thousands of simultaneous users. Organizations like banks and governments use them for transactions, data storage, and large-scale processing.

For example, a bank uses mainframes to process real-time transactions, ensuring accurate and fast services for millions of customers worldwide.


### Source B: The Role of Operating Systems and Applications in Personal Computers
Operating systems like Windows, macOS, and Linux act as the interface between a computer’s hardware and the user. They enable applications—such as word processors, browsers, and gaming software—to run smoothly. The OS manages resources, facilitating multiple applications to work together.

For instance, Windows provides a platform for applications like Microsoft Office and Adobe Photoshop to function efficiently for diverse user needs.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ff2cf25c-433d-47a2-bbad-e28c349ad345)


### Source C: Quantum Computing: The Future of Processing Power
Quantum computing is an emerging area that leverages quantum bits (qubits) to solve problems much faster than classical computers. Quantum computers hold potential for breakthroughs in fields like cryptography and artificial intelligence by handling complex calculations in seconds.

For instance, pharmaceutical companies use quantum simulations to predict molecular interactions, significantly accelerating drug discovery.

### Source D: Input and Output Devices in Modern Computing
Input devices such as keyboards, touchscreens, and voice recognition systems allow users to interact with computers, while output devices like monitors, printers, and VR headsets present information. Input-output functionality enables usability across personal and professional applications.

For example, a virtual reality (VR) system uses motion sensors as input and a VR headset as output, creating an immersive experience for the user.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a58f32c1-c8e5-4011-8519-c052b2e03df2)

questions.specification:
### Source A: Network Security: Protecting Data and Devices
Network security includes tools like firewalls, encryption, and authentication. Firewalls block unauthorized access, while encryption secures data in transit, and authentication verifies user identities. These security measures are essential in corporate and personal networks alike.

For instance, a firewall prevents unauthorized access to a corporate network, and encryption protocols like SSL protect sensitive data during online transactions.



### Source B: Network Services and Applications: From Email to Remote Access
Networks support essential services like email, web browsing, file sharing, and remote access. These services allow data exchange across global networks, supporting both professional and personal communication and collaboration.

For example, SMTP facilitates email transmission, while VPNs enable secure remote access to organizational resources.


### Source C: Understanding Network Addressing: IP and MAC Addresses
Devices on networks are identified by unique IP addresses (for network-level identification) and MAC addresses (for hardware-level identification). Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) assigns IP addresses automatically, while Domain Name System (DNS) translates domain names into IP addresses.

For instance, DNS translates “www.example.com” into an IP address, directing user requests to the appropriate web server.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7e16cb03-871e-4b58-87bc-d771fa273c29)

### Source D: Infrastructure of the Internet: Routers, Switches, and Access Points
The internet relies on routers to direct traffic between networks, switches to connect devices within LANs, and access points to enable Wi-Fi connections. These elements form the backbone of both small networks and the global internet infrastructure.

For example, an office setup includes switches to connect computers, a router to manage internet access, and access points for wireless device connections.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c1f01307-1a1c-4543-8c05-c1f1a469eb65)

questions.specification:
**Source A**

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b479a712-4d14-4664-af63-886fba897137)

**Source B (analysis of BinSync article)** 

BinSync’s pilot operating rules state that routing decisions should be “data-led but reviewable.” The system generates daily routes automatically using sensor readings and prediction models. A supervisor can adjust routes if there is local knowledge (construction, blocked access, or public events). The rules also specify two distinct reporting channels: residents can submit service requests (“missed pickup”) or hazard flags (“overflowing bin attracting animals”). Hazard flags are prioritized, even when the sensor reading is low, because some items (light packaging) can create overflow without a high fill-level. The hotline exists for residents without the app, but callers may receive fewer real-time updates than app users. Drivers must scan a QR code at each pickup; unscanned pickups are treated as incomplete unless a supervisor overrides the record. 

**Source C (community-posted metrics)** 

Missed pickups in the pilot district fell from 7.4 to 4.9 per 1,000 households per week.

Fuel use per tonne collected fell by 11%, but average route length increased by 6% on days after major events.  
15% of bins had at least one “sensor silence” period longer than 6 hours each month; these streets showed higher complaint rates.

Resident reports increased by 33% after push notifications were introduced, with the largest increase in “missed pickup” requests.

The hotline accounts for 19% of reports, but only 6% of “completed” status notifications. 

**Source D (criticism of BinSync in local opinion column)**

BinSync is described as a “smart system,” but it often feels like a system that makes residents do more work. When notifications warn that pickups are delayed, people respond by filing reports, creating the appearance of engagement while quietly shifting responsibility onto the public. The system also privileges what it can measure. A silent sensor becomes a “non-event” until a complaint arrives, and streets where people are less likely to complain can become invisible. The city celebrates fuel savings, but the pilot also shows how fragile the system is: network gaps, QR scans that fail, and event spikes that produce longer routes anyway. Systems thinking should mean looking at the whole loop, including incentives. If supervisors are pressured to trust the routing engine, “reviewable” becomes a slogan rather than a practice. Riverton isn’t just buying sensors; it is building a new set of dependencies between contractors, dashboards, and resident behaviour. Before scaling up, the city should ask: who benefits from the automation, and who carries the cost when the system is wrong?

---

* Resident reporting channels such as an app or hotline and the prioritisation or dispatch system, where reports change service priorities and routing decisions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Network or app infrastructure and real time updates or notifications, where connectivity enables updates and notifications influence reporting behaviour. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Awarding marks**
* Award credit for each correctly identified pair of interdependent elements that shows they function together. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Award a maximum of two. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Marking guidance**
<note>Do not award credit for listing two elements without indicating their interdependence.</note>
<note>Accept any valid pairs supported by the stimulus, not limited to the examples given.</note>

**Sample answer**
The system includes bin fill level sensors that feed data into the route generation model, so collection routes are produced based on current bin status. It also includes driver QR code scanning linked to the completion record system, so scanning updates job status and allows managers to track completed and outstanding tasks.

parts.content: Distinguish between the role of sensor readings and the role of resident reports in BinSync’s decision-making, with reference to Source B.
parts.markscheme:
**Distinguish (Total <marks>4 marks</marks>)**

**Answer points**
* Sensor readings provide automated, continuous or periodic quantitative inputs such as fill level that the system uses to generate daily routes via prediction models, as shown in Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Sensor readings are data-led and form the default basis for routing unless adjusted by supervisors or overridden by report-based priorities in Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Resident reports provide human-generated, contextual information through two channels, service requests and hazard flags, as described in Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Resident reports can override or reprioritise decisions even when sensor readings are low, for example hazard flags prioritised due to potential overflow, according to Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Awarding marks**
* Award for a clear distinction showing how sensor readings differ from resident reports with explicit reference to Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Award for an additional accurate distinction about the role of sensor data in default routing decisions linked to Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Award for a clear distinction about the nature or channels of resident reports linked to Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Award for an additional accurate distinction showing how resident reports can override or reprioritise sensor-based decisions with reference to Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Marking guidance**
<note>Distinctions must compare both sensor readings and resident reports. If a response describes only one side, award a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>. Credit any other valid distinctions that are clearly linked to Source B.</note>

**Sample answer**
Sensor readings differ from resident reports because they are automated and provide continuous quantitative data such as bin fill levels that the system in Source B uses to generate predicted daily routes. In contrast, resident reports come from people and add contextual information through service requests or hazard flags. Sensor data normally forms the default basis for routing, whereas resident reports can override these decisions, for example when a hazard is reported even though the sensor does not show a high fill level.

parts.content: Compare what Source C and Source D suggest about the reliability and fairness of the BinSync pilot.
parts.markscheme:
**Compare [(Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)]**

**Answer points**

* Source C presents reliability as improved performance through fewer missed pickups, while Source D presents reliability as fragility due to network gaps, failed QR scans, and event spikes that lengthen routes, showing contrasting views of what reliability means in practice. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Both sources together suggest that overall reliability gains can exist alongside persistent operational failure points within the same system. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C links sensor silence to higher complaint rates, implying uneven service visibility tied to technical reliability, while Source D argues that streets with fewer complaints become invisible, highlighting shared concerns about fairness. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Both sources imply that unequal reporting capacity and technical gaps can lead to unequal service outcomes across different communities. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C shows increased resident reporting after push notifications, indicating behaviour shaped by system feedback, whereas Source D interprets this as responsibility being shifted onto residents to make problems visible. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Both sources suggest that the system design creates feedback loops that alter citizen behaviour and redistribute burdens within the service model. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Awarding marks**

* Award credit for each effective point of comparison that explicitly refers to both Source C and Source D. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Allow up to a maximum of six for clear, comparative points grounded in evidence from both sources. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Limit responses that focus on only one source to a maximum of three. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Limit responses that describe the sources separately without sustained comparison to a maximum of four. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Marking guidance**

<note>Accept any other valid comparisons that accurately identify similarities or differences between the two sources and are supported by evidence.</note>
<note>Comparisons may be implicit or explicit, but both sources must be referenced within each point.</note>
<note>Do not require equal treatment of all themes, provided comparison is clear and sustained.</note>

**Sample answer**

Both sources discuss reliability, but they frame it differently. Source C shows reliability as improved performance, evidenced by a reduction in missed pickups, whereas Source D argues that the system remains fragile because network gaps and event spikes still disrupt routes. Together, this suggests that efficiency improvements can coexist with underlying weaknesses. The sources also share concerns about fairness. Source C links silent sensors to higher complaints, implying uneven visibility of service problems, while Source D argues that areas with fewer complaints become invisible. Both therefore suggest that who is seen and prioritised depends on technical and social factors. Additionally, Source C shows that push notifications increased reporting, while Source D interprets this as shifting responsibility onto residents, indicating that system design shapes behaviour and redistributes the burden of ensuring service quality.

parts.content: With reference to all the sources (A–D) and your own knowledge of the Digital Society course, evaluate whether Riverton should expand BinSync citywide.
parts.markscheme:
**Evaluate (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**

**Answer points**

* Potential benefits of expansion for effectiveness and efficiency.  
* Risks and limitations for reliability and fairness.  
* Feedback loops and unintended consequences.  
* Group advantage and disadvantage.  
* Overall supported judgement integrating sources and own knowledge.

**Awarding marks**

* Reduced missed pickups and fuel use per tonne indicate improved efficiency and service outcomes from expansion, supporting effectiveness claims from data in Source C. <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* A data-led but reviewable system with supervisor overrides can blend automation with local knowledge, increasing robustness and accountability as suggested in Source B. <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Hazard-flag prioritization can improve safety and public health responsiveness beyond sensor-only logic, strengthening the case for expansion in complex environments. <marks>1 mark</marks>  

* Sensor silence periods and network gaps create blind spots that can lead to uneven service reliability, limiting effectiveness as highlighted in Source C and criticised in Source D. <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* QR-scan failures and strict rules that treat unscanned collections as incomplete can misclassify work and generate disputes, shifting burdens to supervisors and residents as seen in Source B and Source D. <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Event-driven spikes in demand increase route length and reduce efficiency, showing model sensitivity to context and limiting scalability, as noted in Source C and echoed in Source D. <marks>1 mark</marks>  

* Push notifications can increase reporting and responsiveness but may also raise administrative workload and shift responsibility onto residents, creating feedback loops discussed in Sources C and D. <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Differences between app and hotline channels can amplify digital divides, reducing procedural fairness for some users as indicated in Sources B and C. <marks>1 mark</marks>  

* Streets or groups with lower complaint propensity due to language barriers, low trust, or limited app access may be disadvantaged if visibility depends on reports, aligning with Source D and course knowledge on digital inclusion. <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Fewer updates for hotline callers can reduce transparency and perceived fairness, weakening legitimacy of the system according to Sources B and C. <marks>1 mark</marks>  

* A balanced judgement that expansion can improve efficiency and safety if accompanied by safeguards such as audits, multi-channel access, and override mechanisms, integrating source evidence with own knowledge of socio-technical systems. <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Explicit synthesis of multiple sources with relevant own knowledge concepts such as feedback loops or Goodhart’s law to justify the final evaluation. <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Marking guidance**

<note>Credit evaluative responses that weigh benefits against limitations and reach a supported judgement.</note>  
<note>For top marks, expect synthesis of Sources A to D and integration of relevant own knowledge.</note>  
<note>If only sources or only own knowledge are used, award no more than <marks>8 marks</marks>.</note>  
<note>Do not require specific terminology, but concepts must be clearly explained and applied.</note>

**Sample answer**

Expanding the system offers clear efficiency benefits, as reduced missed pickups and lower fuel use per tonne suggest improved service outcomes. The data-led but reviewable design, where supervisors can adjust automated decisions, combines algorithmic efficiency with local knowledge, making the system more robust. Hazard-flag prioritization also strengthens public health and safety responses compared with relying only on sensors.

However, there are significant limitations. Periods of sensor silence and network gaps can create blind spots, leading to uneven service reliability. QR-scan failures and strict rules that treat unscanned collections as incomplete risk misclassification and disputes, shifting extra work onto supervisors and residents. Event-related demand spikes that lengthen routes further show that the model struggles with contextual variation.

Feedback loops add complexity. Push notifications may increase reporting and responsiveness, but they also increase administrative workload and place more responsibility on residents. Differences between app and hotline access can reinforce digital divides, disadvantaging groups less able or willing to report issues, which raises fairness concerns.

Overall, expansion can be justified if strong safeguards are implemented. These include multi-channel access, regular audits, clear appeal pathways, and active supervisor oversight. Without such measures, efficiency gains risk being offset by reliability problems and inequitable outcomes.

questions.specification:
**Social media and political tension**

National governments sometimes block websites at a time of political tension. One example was in 2011, when a government prevented its citizens’ access to _Twitter_, _Facebook_ and _YouTube_. Images on these websites were considered inappropriate, with the potential to cause hatred, violence and political unrest in that country. The national government gave direct orders to the internet service providers (ISPs) to block access to these sites for four hours. The aim was to give enough time for site officials to remove the offending images from their websites.

Despite these attempts by the government, some citizens were still able to access these images using proxy servers.

Many people, such as academics, believe that this approach is not appropriate and that governments should focus on the education of their citizens so that they are able to make informed decisions about how to react to potentially offensive information on websites and social media.

parts.content:
Many schools block access to social networking websites like _Twitter_, _Facebook_ and _YouTube_. However, other schools are investigating two different options:

*   Monitoring the network to view what websites the students are viewing.
*   Giving different ages of students different levels of access to social media sites.

Analyse these **two** options.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Monitoring the network:**

*   It prevents cyberbullying – keep students safe (values).
*   It teaches students responsibility – digital citizenship (values).
*   It protects students from sharing inappropriate information and images about themselves (values).
*   It identifies any objectionable/inappropriate content that students can access on these sites.
*   It gathers data/information for future policies for the use (or not) of social media.
*   Students of all ages would have access to the same websites, regardless of age – younger students could have access to objectionable/inappropriate content for their age.
*   Privacy of students – school would have access to see what the students are viewing.

**Giving different ages different levels of access:**

*   Some websites have age restrictions – should only be accessed if age appropriate (values).
*   Younger students should not be exposed to objectionable/inappropriate content that is acceptable for older students.
*   Schools have a responsibility to ensure the use of the network is appropriate to various ages.
*   Younger students may not be able to differentiate between true intentions of online friendships (expression).
*   Older students need to learn responsible digital citizenship and take responsibility for their actions (values, trust).
*   Awareness of the benefits and drawbacks of social media sites is gained as the student matures (values).
*   Blocking sites by age could cause issues – blocking content that is needed by older students granting different levels of access may be more cost-effective than implementing an ongoing monitoring system (power, values).

  
**Keywords:** home, online, social, relationships, responsibility, trust, software applications, apps, objectionable content, change, expression, identity, power, values, ethics

parts.content:
Many citizens have raised concerns about the surveillance of their web browsing history or the censorship of selected websites by their national government.

To what extent is it appropriate for national governments to use surveillance and censorship to control citizens’ access to websites?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Reasons that surveillance and censorship to control citizens’ access to websites is acceptable:**

*   It can keep children from being victims of cyberbullying, sex trafficking and pornography, objectionable/inappropriate content.
*   It can control/monitor various illegal activities (values).
*   It helps strengthen national security with laws against hacking and impose large fines and punishments (values).
*   It can lessen the incidence of identity theft.
*   Governments may be best informed to determine what may, or may not, be appropriate for their citizens (values).

**Reasons that surveillance and censorship to control citizens’ access to websites is not acceptable:**

*   It removes citizens’ freedom of expression.
*   It can be used to keep information from citizens that may infringe on their privacy and/or anonymity (trust).
*   It can be expensive for the government or may require too many resources for the benefits it brings (systems).
*   It may accidentally block sites that should not be blocked (systems).
*   Knowledge is power.
*   It can be used to identify people who oppose the government (values, expression).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** Automation, connectivity, change, power, systems, values, ethics, machine learning, accountability, transparency, autonomy, surveillance

parts.content: Evaluate the role of sorting algorithms in promoting certain products over others, considering both benefits and ethical concerns for sellers.
parts.markscheme:
### Evaluate the role of sorting algorithms in promoting certain products over others, considering both benefits and ethical concerns for sellers.

Sorting algorithms can be used to promote certain products over others, which has both benefits and ethical concerns for sellers.

**Benefits:**

- Improved user experience: Sorting algorithms can display the most relevant or popular products first, making it easier for customers to find what they are looking for. This enhances the overall shopping experience. <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Increased sales: By promoting certain products, sorting algorithms can drive more sales for those items, benefiting the sellers of those products. <marks>2 marks</marks>

**Ethical Concerns:**

- Unfair competition: Sorting algorithms can give an advantage to larger sellers or those who pay higher fees, making it difficult for smaller sellers to compete and gain visibility. This can lead to a monopolistic market. <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Bias and manipulation: Sorting algorithms can be designed to promote certain products based on factors other than relevance or popularity, such as sponsored listings or partnerships with specific sellers. This can be seen as manipulative and unethical. <marks>2 marks</marks>

<note>Maximum 4 marks for benefits and 4 marks for ethical concerns.</note>

<Marks>8 Total</Marks>

### Types of networks and their purposes

- LAN (Local Area Network) <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Used to connect computers/devices within a limited geographical area such as a home, office, or school

- WAN (Wide Area Network) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The European Union’s (EU) General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) governs data protection and privacy in the EU. Its regulations give users certain rights in terms of their data.

Explain **three** principles that should be included in data protection regulations such as GDPR.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

*   The right to know who has access to my data
*   … and for the purposes it is being used for.  
      
    
*   The level of security used to protect the data
*   … so only appropriate users of the data have access.  
      
    
*   The purpose that the data will be used for
*   … will it be shared with third parties?  
      
    
*   Data will not be processed without informed consent from the owner
*   … unless there is a legal requirement to process the data.  
      
    
*   Requires that the holder of the data controller provides information to the data subject
*   … in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language.  
      
    
*   The data subject has the right to be forgotten
*   … so data can be deleted after it has served its required purpose.  
      
    
*   Data should be stored on a server within the same country or within a country with similar data protection legislation in place.
*   … so that the owner of the data is sure that its security remains protected.

_Award **\[1\]** for identifying a principle that should be included in data regulation principles such as GDPR and **\[1\]** for a development up to **\[2\]**._

_Mark as **\[2\]** **\+ \[2\] + \[2\]**._

parts.content:
A new school has opened in Switzerland, and its principal and IT manager are considering two options:

**Option 1:** Using a local client–server network.

**Option 2:** Using a cloud-based service.

Evaluate these two options.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

**Option 1:**

**Advantages**

*   The management of the client–server network can be carried out in-house which will mean the way in which the network is set up can be tailored to the school’s needs (systems).
*   There will be fewer costs to external agencies for the upkeep of the network i.e. there is no monthly fee / subscription fees, etc., which will need to be managed by the IT manager.
*   The IT manager will have complete knowledge about where their data is held.
*   A client–server network allows in-house control of backup and security (this could be argued from both sides depending on the competency of the IT staff).
*   The principal will be able to claim the data is more secure as it is housed on a school server instead of on a third-party server (systems).

**Disadvantages**

*   The hardware costs for network infrastructure as well as software licensing costs will need to be paid by the school and managed by the IT manager/principal (costs).
*   The IT manager/principal will need to employ staff who can manage the network and maintain the hardware or arrange for this to be outsourced (costs).

**Option 2:**

**Advantages**

*   The management of the client–server network can be carried out remotely by the cloud service provider which will mean that IT manager will not have to carry out a number of tasks linked to the maintenance of the network (feasibility).
*   The cloud-based provider will provide backing up facilities as part of the service so the IT manager will not have to address this issue (reliability, systems).
*   The cloud-based provider will be able to provide up-to-date versions of software and services more efficiently than the IT manager who may have to buy them in after carrying out due diligence (systems).
*   Increasing the storage capacity of the network would be easily done by requesting this from the cloud service provider (systems). No additional hardware or infrastructure will be required (cost, feasibility).

**Disadvantages**

*   There will still be a need to have some hardware and software on site (for example print servers) which will need to be purchased and maintained by the IT manager (cost, systems).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** education, data, security, reliability, network, cloud, client, server, systems, ethics, values, cost, feasibility

questions.specification:
_Google_’s DeepMind uses a machine learning algorithm and neural network to analyse data. _Google_ is now collaborating with the United Kingdom’s National Health Service (NHS) to develop systems that can improve the quality of health care.

Its first project is to create a system that can analyse digital scans of eyes and thus help doctors diagnose sight-destroying diseases in time for them to be treated. This is a complex problem for pattern recognition.

_Google_ needs large quantities of retinal scans to test the algorithm. Moorfields Eye Hospital in London has the largest collection of retinal scans in the world and will share them with _Google_ for free. The scans will be anonymized. Patients can opt out of sharing their data by emailing an NHS data protection officer. _Google_ will use the knowledge gained from this project to help create future software that can eventually be marketed to health care providers.

For this project, _Google_ needs to consider the needs and concerns of multiple stakeholders, including patients, doctors, and the NHS. _Google_ plans to hold four patient forums a year and set up a panel of independent reviewers to oversee what the company is doing. Ultimately, _Google_ hopes to make a profit from this technology.

parts.content:
It is claimed that the NHS’s partnership with _Google_ is beneficial for both stakeholders. However, concerns have been raised about the ethical implications of this data-sharing agreement.

To what extent is it ethical for the NHS to share patient information about eye scans from Moorfields Eye Hospital with _Google_?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Ethical problems:**

*   Can the patients decide whether to opt in or opt out (power)?
*   _Google_ is likely to profit from what they learn from this project.
*   _Google_ will benefit from improvements in its algorithm.
*   _Google_ will need to guarantee that the data is kept secure.
*   _Google_ will need to guarantee that the data is kept private.
*   _Google_ will need to guarantee that the data is only used for this project.
*   Does this scheme cause harm to the patients? Is it ethical?

**Benefits for the NHS and its patients:**

*   _Google_ could provide free access to the system to the NHS vs other health providers.
*   The knowledge gained about preserving sight could be shared with doctors around the world, thereby benefiting many people at risk of losing their sight (values).
*   This data may be shared/mined, and other associations may be established that may lead to preventative health care in unrelated fields.
*   Patients may feel the benefits of sharing their data outweigh the negative implications of their data being shared (values).

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** health, medical care, medical diagnostics, data, privacy, anonymity, algorithm, change, power, systems, values
